title: Te Kaitiaki Hangarau o Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku / The Technology Guardians of Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will explore their role as kaitiaki through the lens of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and holds deep significance for Ngāti Kahungunu. The awa is named for the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks, and students will investigate how technology can help restore and protect this taonga while honoring the pūrākau of our tūpuna who gathered medicinal plants from its waters.
learningObjectives: Design and prototype a simple water quality testing device using digital and material technologies to monitor Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
Create a digital storymap that layers mātauranga Māori about the awa with scientific data collected through technological monitoring
Collaboratively develop a technology-based solution addressing one identified threat to the health of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
Present technological findings and proposals using appropriate te reo Māori terminology and cultural protocols to community stakeholders

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Visit Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua to hear pūrākau, learn traditional names and uses of plants along its banks, and observe current conditions. Students use digital cameras and audio recorders to document observations and stories.

activities.title: He Rauemi Hangarau mō te Tātari Wai
activities.description: Workshop to design and build simple water testing tools using micro:bit sensors, pH testing strips, and turbidity tubes. Students calibrate devices and develop digital recording methods for water quality data.

activities.title: Te Hōpara Matihiko
activities.description: Using GIS mapping software and traditional Māori mapping knowledge, students create layered digital maps showing historical significance, current environmental data, and potential restoration sites along the awa.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Hangarau
activities.description: Design challenge where groups develop technological solutions (e.g., native planting drone seed dispersal, smart litter collection systems, digital monitoring network) to address specific issues identified in their research.

activities.title: Te Whakaatu mai i te Hōtaka
activities.description: Students present their technological solutions and findings to whānau, community members, and local iwi representatives using digital presentations that incorporate both technical data and cultural knowledge.

resources: Micro:bit classroom sets with water quality sensors
Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Rangiātea Whaanga as cultural guide
NIWA RiverWatch water monitoring protocols and digital tools
Te Ara - The Encyclopedia of New Zealand online resources on Māori environmental knowledge

tasks: Design portfolio documenting the technological development process
Functional prototype of water monitoring device or restoration technology
Digital storymap presentation integrating cultural and scientific data
Oral presentation demonstrating use of te reo Māori technical terms

nzcAlignment: Technological Practice: Planning for practice, brief development, outcome development and evaluation
Technological Knowledge: Understanding technological modelling and products
Nature of Technology: Characteristics of technology and characteristics of technological outcomes
Social Sciences: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
